title: Real Return Calculator: Inflation-Adjusted Returns
description: Calculate your true investment return after inflation. See how purchasing power erodes over time with our real vs nominal return calculator.

schemas.headline: Real Return Calculator: Inflation-Adjusted Returns
schemas.description: Calculate your true investment return after inflation. See how purchasing power erodes over time with our real vs nominal return calculator.

mainEntity.name: How do you calculate real return?

acceptedAnswer.text: Real return is calculated using the Fisher equation: ((1 + Nominal Return) / (1 + Inflation Rate)) - 1. For example, a 10% nominal return with 3% inflation yields a real return of 6.8% (1.10 / 1.03 - 1), not 7%.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between real and nominal return?

acceptedAnswer.text: Nominal return is the raw percentage increase in your investment's value. Real return is the true increase in your purchasing power, adjusted for the effects of inflation.

mainEntity.name: What is a good real rate of return?

acceptedAnswer.text: Historically, the stock market (S&P 500) has provided an annualized real rate of return of about 6% to 7% after inflation over long periods. A real return of 4% to 5% is generally considered a conservative target for a diversified portfolio.

mainEntity.name: Why does inflation matter for investments?

acceptedAnswer.text: Inflation erodes the purchasing power of your money over time. If your investment grows at 5% but inflation is 6%, you have actually lost 1% in purchasing power, making your true (real) return negative.
Equities (stocks), real estate, and TIPS (Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities) have historically been the most effective asset classes for beating inflation over the long term.
